questions.question: How many triggers can a single Azure Function have, and are bindings required?
options: Exactly one trigger; bindings are optional
One or more triggers; bindings are required
Exactly one trigger; at least one input binding is required
Zero or more triggers; bindings are optional
questions.explanation: A function has exactly one trigger (what causes it to run), and bindings are optional. If you need several event sources you write several functions; an input binding is never mandatory.

questions.question: A Function App is the unit of deployment and scale: all functions in the app share the same host process and plan and scale together.
questions.explanation: The Function App is the deployment/scale unit. All functions in it share configuration, runtime, and plan, and scale out together as a whole app.

questions.question: Which statement correctly describes input versus output bindings?
options: Input bindings read data into the function; output bindings write data out of it
Input bindings write results out; output bindings read source data in
Both input and output bindings only read data; the trigger writes
Input bindings invoke the function; output bindings cannot supply data
questions.explanation: Input bindings declaratively read data into the function (e.g. a blob or Cosmos lookup); output bindings write data out (e.g. a queue message or table row).

questions.question: What is the default and maximum function execution timeout on the Consumption plan?
options: 5 minutes default, 10 minutes maximum
1 minute default, 5 minutes maximum
10 minutes default, 30 minutes maximum
5 minutes default, unbounded maximum
questions.explanation: On Consumption the default timeout is 5 minutes and the maximum is 10 minutes. Effectively unbounded runs require Premium/Dedicated with functionTimeout configured.

questions.question: For modern .NET Azure Functions, which execution model is the recommended path forward, and what is the key reason to migrate off the other?
options: Isolated worker; in-process support ends Nov 10, 2026
In-process; isolated worker support ends Nov 10, 2026
Isolated worker; in-process has higher per-execution billing
In-process; isolated worker cannot use dependency injection
questions.explanation: The isolated worker model is the path forward; in-process .NET support ends Nov 10, 2026. Isolated also gives runtime independence, own DI/middleware, and avoids assembly-version conflicts.

questions.question: Which of the following are genuine advantages of the isolated worker model over in-process? (Select all that apply.)
options: It decouples your assembly/runtime from the host, avoiding assembly-version conflicts
It lets you own Program.cs with a custom middleware pipeline and DI lifecycle
It can run .NET versions decoupled from the host, including .NET Framework 4.8
It has lower cold start than in-process
It gives direct access to rich host types like HttpRequest and IActionResult
questions.explanation: Isolated decouples the runtime (avoiding assembly conflicts), gives full Program.cs/middleware/DI control, and can target versions including .NET Framework 4.8. It has slightly HIGHER cold start, and rich host types like HttpRequest/IActionResult belong to the in-process model (isolated uses HttpRequestData/HttpResponseData).

questions.question: You need serverless economics (scale to zero, pay-per-use) PLUS VNet integration and always-ready instances to eliminate cold start. Which hosting plan is the modern recommendation?
options: Flex Consumption
Consumption
Dedicated (App Service) with Always On
Container Apps
questions.explanation: Flex Consumption is the modern default: serverless economics with VNet integration and always-ready instances to kill cold start. Plain Consumption lacks VNet and warm instances; Dedicated does not scale to zero.

questions.question: Why is instance count alone an incomplete picture of a Function App's throughput?
options: Each instance runs many invocations in parallel, so throughput = instances × per-instance concurrency
Instances are billed per-second, so count only affects cost, not throughput
The scale controller caps every instance to exactly one invocation at a time
Throughput is determined solely by the storage account's IOPS, not instances
questions.explanation: Throughput equals instances multiplied by per-instance concurrency. Each instance processes many invocations concurrently, so tuning maxConcurrentCalls/batchSize matters as much as how many instances exist.

questions.question: Most Azure Functions triggers deliver at-least-once. What is the correct way to handle the resulting risk of duplicate processing?
options: Make the handler idempotent (dedup on a stable id, upserts, or conditional/ETag writes)
Disable retries so a message is only ever delivered once
Rely on host-level in-process retry policies to guarantee exactly-once
Set maxDeliveryCount to 1 so duplicates are impossible
questions.explanation: You cannot prevent at-least-once redelivery, so handlers must be idempotent (stable-id dedup, upserts, conditional/ETag writes). In-process retry policies are best-effort, not a delivery or exactly-once guarantee, and lowering maxDeliveryCount does not stop redelivery before that limit.

questions.question: After repeated failures, where do failed messages go for Service Bus versus Azure Storage queues?
options: Service Bus dead-letters after maxDeliveryCount; Storage queues move to a `<queue>-poison` queue
Both go to a `<queue>-poison` queue
Service Bus moves to `<queue>-poison`; Storage queues dead-letter
Both are dropped silently once maxDeliveryCount is exceeded
questions.explanation: Service Bus has a dead-letter queue (DLQ) reached after maxDeliveryCount; Azure Storage queues move the message to a `<queue>-poison` queue. They are durable destinations, not silent drops.

questions.question: When would you drop from declarative bindings down to using the SDK directly in code? (Select all that apply.)
options: You need multi-message transactions across entities
You need streaming of large payloads or custom retry/backoff
You need a feature newer than what the binding exposes
You want the host to manage connection pooling and batching for you
You want plain types so the function body is easy to unit-test
questions.explanation: Use the SDK for control bindings don't expose: cross-entity transactions, streaming, custom retry, dynamic targets, or newer features. Host-managed connection pooling/batching and easy-to-test plain types are advantages of bindings, not reasons to abandon them.

questions.question: The classic polling blob trigger on Consumption can miss blobs at high scale, so the Event Grid-based blob trigger source is the recommended production option for low latency and reliable delivery.
questions.explanation: The classic blob trigger polls the container (latency and possible misses at high scale). The Event Grid-based source pushes events, giving low latency and reliable delivery, and is the recommended production approach.

questions.question: Why is it a problem to `new` up an SDK client (e.g. a Service Bus or HTTP client) per function invocation under scale, and what is the fix?
options: It exhausts sockets/connections; register the client as a singleton and reuse it
It increases GB-second billing; create the client in a static constructor per call
It breaks idempotency; create a fresh client for every retry
It forces a cold start on each call; disable connection pooling
questions.explanation: Creating a client per invocation exhausts sockets/connections at scale. Register clients as singletons (e.g. via AddAzureClients/DI) and reuse them — which is exactly what bindings already do for you.

questions.question: An orchestrator function behaves inconsistently across runs. It calls `Guid.NewGuid()` and branches on `DateTime.UtcNow.Hour`. What is the root cause and the correct fix?
options: Both calls are non-deterministic and break replay; use `ctx.NewGuid()` and `ctx.CurrentUtcDateTime`
The orchestrator is missing an [ActivityTrigger]; add one to make it deterministic
Guid.NewGuid() is fine, but DateTime.UtcNow must be awaited as an activity
Both are fine; the inconsistency is caused by missing retry policies on the activities
questions.explanation: Orchestrators are replayed from history on every await, so Guid.NewGuid() and DateTime.UtcNow produce different values each replay, corrupting the workflow. Use the deterministic ctx.NewGuid() and ctx.CurrentUtcDateTime; any real randomness or I/O must move into an activity.

questions.question: In Durable Functions, where do side effects (I/O, external calls) belong, and how are Durable Entities processed?
options: Side effects belong in activity functions; each entity processes one message at a time (serialized)
Side effects belong in the orchestrator; entities process messages in parallel for throughput
Side effects belong in the client function; entities are stateless and non-addressable
Side effects belong in activities; entities process all messages concurrently per key
questions.explanation: Activities are the only place side effects belong (orchestrators must stay deterministic and side-effect-free). Durable Entities hold addressable per-key state and process one operation at a time, serialized per entity.

questions.question: In this Durable Functions snippet, what does the framework guarantee about the parallel work, and what is `Task.WhenAll` providing here?
options: It is fan-out/fan-in: activities run in parallel and the framework durably tracks completion across instance recycles, with WhenAll as the durable join
It runs the activities strictly sequentially; WhenAll just collects the return values
It runs the activities on the client function and blocks the orchestrator thread until all finish
Task.WhenAll forces all activities onto a single instance to preserve ordering
questions.explanation: Adding tasks without awaiting fans out the CopyFile activities in parallel; await Task.WhenAll is the fan-in/durable join. The framework durably tracks completion even across instance recycles, so progress survives restarts.

questions.question: Scaling a Function App out to many instances breaks global message ordering. Which approach preserves ordering while still allowing some parallelism?
options: Use Service Bus sessions (one session processed by one instance at a time) or Event Hubs partitions
Raise maxConcurrentCalls so each instance processes messages faster in arrival order
Switch to Storage queues, which guarantee strict global FIFO across instances
Set the functions scale limit to its maximum so all instances see the same order
questions.explanation: Scaling out breaks global ordering. Service Bus sessions (one session per instance at a time) or Event Hubs partitions preserve per-session/per-partition order while still parallelizing across keys. Raising concurrency or switching to Storage queues does not provide ordering guarantees.

questions.question: A queue-triggered function scales to ~200 instances and overwhelms a backing SQL database. Without removing the function, which measures protect the database? (Select all that apply.)
options: Lower per-instance concurrency via batchSize/maxConcurrentCalls in host.json
Set a functions scale limit to cap the maximum number of instances
Apply resiliency patterns toward the DB (retry with backoff, circuit breaker, bulkhead) with a bounded connection pool
Increase per-instance concurrency so each instance finishes faster and disconnects sooner
Remove all retry policies so failed calls are dropped immediately
questions.explanation: Throughput = instances × concurrency, so lowering per-instance concurrency, capping instance count with a scale limit, and adding bounded pooling plus resiliency patterns all reduce load on the DB. Increasing concurrency makes the overload worse, and dropping retries harms correctness without protecting the database.
